scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text describes a specific Roman custom concerning the religious purity and execution of Vestal Virgins during the Second Punic War era.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The execution of Vestal Virgins like Floronia and Opimia occurred in -216 BCE during the consulship of Fabius Maximus as recounted in the work.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule describes Roman military practice regarding the treatment of captured populations in southern Italy (Tarentum).
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The capture of Tarentum by Fabius Maximus occurred specifically in -209 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The verbatim describes the formal summons procedure between Roman magistrates (a consul and his father) using lictors.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This incident occurred in -213 BCE when the son of Fabius Maximus was consul and the father served as his legate.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text explicitly discusses the Roman legal norm where consular authority overrides the traditional 'patria potestas'.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This exchange took place in -213 BCE during the younger Fabius's consulship.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The custom described is the Roman aristocratic practice of delivering a 'laudatio funebris' for distinguished family members.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The death and funeral of the son of Fabius Maximus occurred around -207 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text identifies a 'religious custom' or lex sacra governing the movements of the Roman Pontifex Maximus.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This specific incident regarding Licinius Crassus being held back by religious taboo refers to events around -241 BCE (or his later equivalent in -205 BCE).

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The verbatim specifically contrasts Fabius with Pericles' conduct in 5th-century Athens, citing the Athenian practice of ostracism.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The ostracism of Thucydides son of Melesias, mentioned in the text, occurred in -443 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule describes the civic policy of Pericles in providing festivals to the Athenian citizenry during his administration.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This represents the height of Pericles' domestic program in Athens during the mid-5th century BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The passage highlights Fabius's personal action in accordance with Roman norms for ransoming citizens captured by Hannibal.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The ransom of prisoners by Fabius Maximus took place following the Battle of Lake Trasimene in -217 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: This reflects the social and legal conflicts between patricians and plebeians in early 5th-century Rome regarding debt.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Coriolanus's political activity and the debt crises peaked around -491 BCE during the first plebeian secession.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text groups Metellus, Aristides, and Epaminondas, but the primary reference to 'the people's power' to outcome elections and ostracize is rooted in Athenian democracy.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Midpoint of the work's span (-500 to -404 BCE) which covers the height of these democratic powers.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The reference to the people's power to condemn in courts describes the Athenian 'dikasteria' system.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Midpoint of the work's span (-500 to -404 BCE) representing the classical period of Athenian legal sovereignty.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text describes the customary duty and political behavior of the city-state of Corinth toward its colonies.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The Corinthian embassy and decision to aid Syracuse took place in -344 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule details the land redistribution laws set for the resettlement of Syracuse which favored specific Greek ethnic groups.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The land allotment and invitation to exiles occurred following the liberation of Syracuse in -343 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Timoleon explicitly restores/establishes the 'right of free speech' (parrhesia) for Syracusan citizens under the law.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This incident involving Demaenetus and the defense of free speech occurred late in Timoleon's career, around -338 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The verbatim describes a formal decree passed by the Syracusan assembly regarding the selection of their military leadership.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This decree was established toward the end of Timoleon's life or as part of his institutional legacy around -337 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule concerns the state-honored burial and the establishment of the Timoleonteum in the Syracusan market place.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Timoleon died and was granted this public funeral in -337 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Description of the Solonian property qualifications or 'tele' for the Athenian archonship.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aristides held the archonship in -489 BCE under these property qualifications.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text describes the social targets of the Athenian institution of ostracism.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aristides himself was ostracised in -482 BCE, an event the text uses to illustrate this rule.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Outlines the specific quorum and counting procedures for ostracism in the Athenian Agora.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Midpoint of the work's span (-530 to -468 BCE) representing the operative period of the Cleisthenic ostracism laws.
